career.notes: International career with Mobil Oil — postings in Germany, Switzerland, Italy, and Japan during 1947-1975 marriage to Joan.
Brought chess programs to inner-city schools.
Delegate to FIDE (World Chess Federation). Led opposition to FIDE leadership at infamous 1994 Moscow Congress.

military_service.notes: First Lieutenant in US Army paratroops; awarded Bronze Star.

narrative_blocks.header: Born the same day his mother died
narrative_blocks.body: Faneuil 'Fan' Adams Jr. was born July 20, 1923 in Boston — the same day his mother Rose Bradley Adams died, almost certainly in childbirth. He was the only documented son of Benjamin Faneuil Dunkin Adams II by Rose, his first wife. The same-day birth-and-mother-death is one of the more piercing first-day biographies in the tree, alongside a sibling in another branch's brother who lived only one day.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII paratroop Bronze Star, then Korean War service
narrative_blocks.body: Fan Adams served as a U.S. Army First Lieutenant in paratroops during World War II, receiving the Bronze Star. He then served again in the Korean War — making him a two-conflict combat veteran. He spent his postwar career as a Mobil Oil executive from 1947 to 1975, with overseas postings in Germany, Switzerland, Italy, and Japan, before retiring to devote himself to chess advocacy.

narrative_blocks.header: 1994 FIDE Moscow Congress resignation, vindicated election
narrative_blocks.body: As a U.S. Chess Federation Policy Board member and delegate to FIDE, Fan attended the 1994 FIDE Moscow Congress. Under USCF Board pressure to reverse position and support FIDE leadership's heavy-handed tactics, he resigned his Board seat on the spot rather than comply — and within a year was overwhelmingly re-elected to the same Board he had just resigned from. He had also served as President of Chess in Schools, the inner-city programs organization, and Treasurer of the Manhattan Chess Club.

quotes.text: Fan Adams was a man of integrity. When he was the delegate of the United States Chess Federation to the infamous 1994 FIDE Congress at Moscow his integrity shone through. At that Congress, Fan led the battle against the heavy handed and illegal tactics employed by the World Chess Federation's leadership in order for them to stay in power. Rather than go against his principles, Fan resigned on the spot from a job he cherished.
quotes.attribution: Don Schultz, eulogy for Faneuil Adams Jr., NYC, April 1, 1999
